speaker: interviewer
text: Today is October 29, 1995. I'm conducting an interview with Morris Price. The interviewer-- Ita Gordon. The city is Los Angeles, California. The language is English. I'm Ita Gordon. I-T-A G-O-R-D-O-N. I'm conducting an interview with Morris Price. Today is October 29, 1995. We are in Culver City, California, USA.

speaker: interviewee
text: Your name-- tell me your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Morris Price. P-R-I-C-E First name, Morris, M-O-R-R-I-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moishe, spelled M-O-I-S-H-E, Prajs P-R-A-J-S

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your Polish name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moishe Prajs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- I had two names in Poland. Moishe was my Yiddish, and my Polish name when I went to public school, was Moniek, M-O-N-I-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Wolbrom W-O-L-B-R-O-M, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is Wolbrum approximately?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolbrum is right now is called, Katowice [POLISH] Katowice. This is about 60 kilometers from Wolbrum. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the next big town close to Wolbrum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the other big town besides Katowice is Krakow. And Krakow is about 50 kilometers from Wolbrum.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1927, on April 1st.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are now how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm now 68 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 68 Years old. What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Manela, M-A-N-E-L-A Manella, and Price also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Businessman. Basically like dealing in cattle, not quite like the state's cattle. [LAUGHS] Nothing that large of a size, you know. Much smaller weight.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Itka. I-T-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kuhl. K-U-H-L. Kuhl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Price was her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Last name was Price, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have-- I had three-- two brothers and three sisters. One of my brothers and one of my sisters were lost during the Holocaust. But I have one brother and two sisters alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me all of their names, like the oldest to the youngest?

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother is [NON-ENGLISH], also Price. He spells it a little different, the last name. He spells it P-R-E-I-S, like in German-- because we were in Germany for a while. And he lives in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is he approximately? Or when was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: He is 84 years old. He was born in 1911.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then is my sister-- which, her name is Sabina. And she's married. Her married name is Opperman, O-P-P-E-R-M-A-N. And she lives in Culver City.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my next sister is Helen. And she's also married. And her married name is Rubin, R-U-B-I-N. And she lives in New Jersey.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born in 1920.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My other sister which is older was born in 1924. And my sister that-- my brother-- well-- let's see.

speaker: interviewer
text: There's a brother? Another brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah,no-- just one brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: One brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. But in Europe, did you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had another brother and his name was David. And he would have been born in 1922. And my sister, another sister that I lost, her name was [NON-ENGLISH], and she was born 1913.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you in all-- were you the youngest one then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I'm the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're the baby. [LAUGHS] And did you have any grandparents that lived nearby?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The only grandparent that I remember was my grandmother. And she was my mother's mother. And she was alive when the Holocaust started, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a relationship with your grandmother? Do you remember her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. She used to say-- when in Poland, when I used to get-- wanted to go ice skating, I didn't have ice skate like here and the snow and everything. And we used to put on to some handmade skates. And she used to say, how-- I can't stand on my feet-- how can you stand on your ice skates? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me a little bit about Wolbrum. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town. It was a large Jewish population. And I had a good time over there. I had a good time. I had parents, you know. When it was-- when I was on vacation from school, my dad used to take me-- we used to go on some business trips. I met some people that he used to do business with. And I had a great time.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the surrounding areas of Wolbrum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your house like? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- we had our own house. I guess the house was large, because there was nine of us in the house, you know. We had, I think, three rooms and a kitchen. We didn't all have our own bedrooms. It was more like two in the room, or three in the room, and everything. And I remember telling my wife when we got married and had kids, and she wanted to have everyone in a room, you know. And I told, I said, if we keep them in the same room, they might know each other better, might get along better than separated. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And the streets, what were the streets like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's cobblestone. There wasn't-- cement and cobblestones.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My dad was-- we were Orthodox. I mean not strictly, but a kosher home, and my dad would never do anything on Shabbos and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a synagogue in Wolbrum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was two. One was called a synagogue and one-- not a cheder-- it was something else, you know, where my dad was more religious-- like orthodox-- a shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: A shul.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's it-- a shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you belonged to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We belonged. My dad used to read the Torah every Saturday or every holidays.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you belonged to the shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your family celebrate the holidays? Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- we really loved the holidays, you know. We had a great time at the holidays. It was-- we even had-- you know-- my dad been doing business with farmers, we always used to-- when it came before wintertime-- or wintertime-- we used to have a lot of apples in the attic, and we had a basement. We had a great time.

speaker: interviewer
text: I meant to ask your mother's occupation before. Can you tell me what her occupation was?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was basically a housewife. Yeah, basically a housewife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what she prepared? Anything-- food that stayed with you that you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food? Well we used to-- oh, yeah. We had-- she used to buy meat. And then once in a while, even like-- we used to have some lamb. And my dad used to buy a whole lamb. And we had the [YIDDISH] We had-- yeah, there was really plenty of meat. And-- and then, since I was the youngest-- this I remember-- and we used to eat late dinner, because a lot of time we used to wait till Dad comes home. And when I got home from school, they used to give me, I think, six groschen and I used to buy [NON-ENGLISH] of salami, of kosher salami. Well, there wasn't any other salami-- it's kosher salami. And I used to have something like a snack before dinner or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's groschen? You mentioned groschen. What is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's like 20 cents-- like six cents. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went to two. I went to public school in the daytime, and cheder in the afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned cheder. What is a cheder? Can you explain?

speaker: interviewee
text: A cheder is where Jewish kids go to-- for religious-- religion and Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this cheder? Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The cheder was, I think, almost in the shul. I don't remember. It was near the shul or something like that. Yeah it was near the shul, was the cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who taught you at the cheder?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi. Or-- was-- sometimes you were with the rabbi-- no, it was a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the teacher from Wolbrum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in school, did you have many friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I don't think I had that-- yeah. I had lots of friends in school.

words.text: I don't think I had that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Public school, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a lot of friends who were both Jewish and non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Jewish. Maybe some non-Jewish too, because it was public. But most of them was Jewish friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever sense any anti-Semitism at that point, while you were going to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that age. I was kind of young. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your parents talking about anti-Semitism at home at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes-- and Poland was anti-Semitic, but you know, it-- it was-- I don't know how much I was aware of it and things like that, but it was anti-Semitic, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you observe any squirmishes or with friends, between young Jewish students and non-Jewish students? Did you observe any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. No, not really, no. At that time, no, I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you pass your time in Wolbrum?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, between public school in the morning and cheder in the afternoon, and maybe studying a little bit for public school, it was a full day of schooling.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for fun?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we played-- the kids, between some of the friends-- we used to go play soccer-- you know-- just-- not professional. I played a little bit in school, too, when they had a team, but we used to like kid around and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have anymore memories of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well in the summer, there was a lake. We used to go swimming. In fact, I learned how to swim in the lake. Nobody taught me, but either you drown or you swim. [LAUGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a happy childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. In fact, we had-- you know, my dad used to-- we had a horse, you know, that he used to-- like a-- like a cart to go, horse and wagon-- and in the summer, when my dad wasn't there, we used to take the horse to the lake. We used to-- I used to ride the horse. Yeah, it was lots of fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do like during-- in terms of the diet, is what I would like to ask, what kind of diet did you have? And the kind of foods that you had in Poland at that time. What was the main diet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meat. Lots of potatoes, and bread. Just like regular-- we had plenty of meat. We had plenty of food. I mean we weren't wealthy, but we had plenty, being that the situation with my-- because my dad was doing business with farmers, there was always eggs, butter, cheese, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What relationship-- the relationship with neighbors-- were they mostly Jewish neighbors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we had-- in fact, our house was like in the back. And we had like two or three people, two neighbors that lived in the front-- that had a house in the front, us in the back. We had a very good relationship, very good friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first notice that things were changing?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, when Germans occupied Poland in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And until then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until then, well, I heard my dad, you know, that-- you know, he had a beard and everything, and a lot of times, you know, when he was coming home, traveling from sometimes, that maybe he might have been afraid, maybe, that some of the Polacks know he was Jewish, like anti-Semitic, something like that. But not really, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you belong to any youth organizations? Any clubs?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, what do you remember about when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war broke out, everything has changed. I couldn't go to school anymore, because, I guess, being Jewish. couldn't go to public school. My dad, you know, had a beard, so they tried to take all eligible male-- you know they're afraid-- put in camps and things like that. So my dad didn't go out of the house. My two brothers-- one of them was in the Polish army. And one of them had run away from home because the Germans came close-- I don't know where he was running. But he ran away. And-- and I had to start-- if we had to go someplace, I was the one that was going. You know, like we had found out by the third or the fourth day, that a camp nearby that was basically like a holding camp where they had a lot of people-- Jewish people-- from our town, our hometown. And so some of the people from our hometown wanted to go to visit them. And that was about 15 kilometers. It's a long way to walk. And since we had a horse and wagon, we took about-- I don't know how many people-- a couple more people in from the town, where they had transportation. And we went to take these people to their town with some bread and things like that, and to see if any of your relatives are there, or any of your family. And also give them some food. And when I was there, I found out that my brother was-- had-- was in that camp. And I gave him some food. And also, at that time, there was a curfew. You had to be in by a certain-- 6 o'clock, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1939, right when the Germans occupied Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was just like-- it wasn't existing very long. It was just like maybe a holding camp. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember of the day the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember planes. And bombs-- not that much, because it was a small town. And the next thing I remember, the German army marching in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see any German officers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The German army marching in, I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. Not at that particular time. No-- not myself at that particular time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to that camp where your brother was sent--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. When I got to that camp-- like I didn't finish-- I had some bread with me, you know. And when I found out that I was-- my brother was there, so I went-- I don't know how-- I don't know exactly-- but I went to bring him some bread. And when I got back to where the horse and buggy was there to go back to our town, the other two or three people that we were almost like in a caravan, they had left because they couldn't wait for me, because they had to get back before the curfew was up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I was getting-- so when I got back to-- the horse was there. The buggy was a little late. And I was trying to go as fast as the horse could go to get back. And when the other people got back, I remember my mom or something, they said, where's Moishe? Where's Moishe? And they said, well, we didn't see him when we got back to go back. We didn't see him. And we had to go back. The horse was there and the buggy was there, so I got the probably just in the nick and the wood. And they were worried, scared what happened to me? I was only 12 years old, you know. And this was right after the war had broken out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Right after the war. And I was actually, right after the war, I was the only one that-- you know, whatever you did was illegal. To eat-- anything you did was illegal. So if I got caught it was bad. But otherwise, I used to like use the horse and buggy to transport some flowers to this, and this to that, you know. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your brother. What's his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mahel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mahel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And then my other brother, which was Dovet-- and they were-- he came back. He was in the army. He was in a prisoner camp for a while. Then he came back, you know. And then, we lived there at-- in home. At that time-- whatever you-- they still were going some kind little business, you know. It wasn't-- you weren't supposed to do nothing, but you're still doing something, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that if you did anything-- what were the consequences? Did you observe or witness--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, they were constantly-- they were constantly rounding up eligible people for work camps, to send you here, to send you there. So basically, you didn't go for walks in the street, you know. If you had to do something, you did. But you were in constant awareness that you're not supposed to be outside. You're not supposed to do this, or you're not supposed to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find out about the existence of all these different camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the one camp, at the beginning. The other camps, actually, we didn't know until was actually sent away till-- till your sent-- till liquidated and sent to one of the camps, you know. And they had a lot of work, that if they caught you, even if they didn't send you to camp, they had some work in town, around the town. Have-- you know-- took you for a week or two weeks, and do some physical labor, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was everybody at home still at that moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. My both brothers-- and my mother and father, my three sisters. One of my sisters, oldest sister, which is not survived, she even got married during the war. But they were all still home, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was life like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't-- no life. It was just like existence, and hoping the war is going to end, and things are going to get better. And you were just telling yourself that it's got to improve. You-- I later on found out that there's no limit to the good, and there's no limit to the bad. But at that time, we were always hoping, things are going to get better, because you said, this is the worst. This can't get any worse, can't get any worse. But it got worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: How? Because when-- by the time you got 1942, they liquidated the whole town. We had heard about liquidation, ghettos, things like that. But since it was a small town-- I guess they started with the largest cities first, and then they came down to the smaller. And then they liquidated the whole town. And one of my two sisters, they went in hiding at the-- on a farm, at the farmers. And the rest of us, where we were liquidated, was my-- we were taken to an assembly into town-- a big field, like a football field, with all your belongings, whatever you could carry. And from there, they were sorted out. My two brothers and myself were sent to one camp. My mother, and my grandmother, and my sister was sent to another camp. My dad was sent to another camp. We wound up-- my two brothers and myself-- wound up in a camp which was called, Prokocim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that, Prokocim?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was near Krakow. It was just maybe 8 kilometers, or something, 8, 10 kilometers outside of Krakow. It was a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going? Did they tell you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. In fact, we got there by train. And from train-- from the train to the camp, I don't know, it was a forced marched. I don't know how many kilometers, one or two or three. And I remember I tripped and I fell. And my brother-- my brother grabbed me and picked me up. And when we got to that camp, you know, we had to line up, and line up fast and everything. And somebody didn't line up. And they wanted you to know that you're in a camp. And one man didn't line up so good, and he was shot right in front of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You witnessed that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was shot right in front of us, to make you think, I mean, you better watch out or you're going to be next.

speaker: interviewer
text: What brothers were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mahel and Dovet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were with you at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was that train like, that took you to Prokocim?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a short distance. It was just like a freight train, yeah, short distance.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people approximately, do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: In camp?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no. On the train going--

speaker: interviewee
text: On the train we were just herded in like cattle. They didn't want to leave any space, you know. They didn't give me you a chair to sit on, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just wanted to ask you what you remember of the last day at home before you were sent to the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing pleasant. Nothing pleasant, because I know one day, I was-- even myself at that age, I was caught-- and I was for labor and before they did that, I was in jail for a day. You know-- the first time I was ever in jail, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What jail was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, you know like-- it was a regular jail. You know, I remember-- there for a day in jail, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you to Prokocim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Basically if we had any money, we took it with us. You took some clothes, some food, some money, and that's about it. You couldn't-- if you had, maybe take a backpack or a piece of luggage, something like that. The-- the closest thing you can grab.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your next memory of Prokocim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Prokocim, I-- they were-- I was working on the camp, and they were doing rail work. They were laying tracks, like railroad tracks. And naturally, I was young and I wasn't able to lift up the big sledge hammers to do something like that. So I was doing maybe helping with the gravel or cleanup and things like that. And we were there for-- and that still a labor camp so you still were allowed to have some money. You didn't have the barbed wires and things like that. And I was there for about-- I don't know-- four weeks or something. And after that, we-- oh, while we were-- while were there, four weeks you know, something like that.
